title: Quadrupole CT-Selective Pulse Width Optimization
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"36ter","text":"Purpose","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":7}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"daqoo","text":"Calibration of central-transition-selective 90° pulse widths on quadrupolar nuclei.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3jm39","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ashtg","text":"Scope","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":5}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1mg3d","text":"This protocol is intended for determining and optimizing central-transition-selective pulses, which is only possible in quadrupolar systems. Not every experiment requires a CT-selective pulse, and so this protocol should only used in cases where CT-selective pulses are required. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"ejleq","text":"In the ideal limit, a CT-selective pulse will be shorter than a non-selective 90° pulse of the same wattage by a factor of  , where I is the nuclear spin number. In practice, CT-selective pulses are often required to have low radiofrequency power and therefore are long. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":132,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":123,"length":1}],"data":{}},{"key":"665c2","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4h1q2","text":"CT-selective pulses are inherently dependent on the quadrupolar coupling constant of the site of interest in ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"khk5","text":"the sample of interest, and thus cannot be reliably optimized to a high level of accuracy on a standard sample. Starting from a standard sample with small υQ can allow for faster empirical optimization of a CT-selective pulse, however.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":155,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":156,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bib3a","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5vo0t","text":"CT-selective pulses should fulfill the condition υrf≪υQ. For environments where υQ≈ 0, e.g., 23Na in NaCl, a CT-selective pulse will not be achievable.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":49,"length":1},{"style":"italic","offset":53,"length":1},{"style":"italic","offset":80,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":50,"length":2},{"style":"sub","offset":54,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":81,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":93,"length":2}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"tex_formula","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"formula":"1/(I+1/2)","guid":"","id":0}}}}

steps: Acquire an initial spectrum using a previously optimized non-selective pulse.
Measure the frequency range of the CT peaks.
Choose a 90° pulse width with a sufficient excitation bandwidth to cover the CT peaks. 5–10 μs pulse lengths are typically acceptable.
Using the pulse au program, calculate the power level plw1 corresponding to the desired pulse length p1 . Set p1 and plw1 accordingly.
Array pulse width p1 , ensuring that the array covers 2 × p1 , where p1 is the value determined in Step 5.
Run the popt and observe the nutation curve.
Determine the 90° pulse length by finding the pulse length corresponding to the 180° zero crossing point and dividing it by 2.
If a more accurate determination is required, popt can be run again with revised start, end, and step values. go to step #8
Set p1 to the 90° value determined in Step 10.
Zoom the spectrum to the peak of interest. Run the command dpl to set the popt window spacing.
creator: NMRFAM Facility
